item.title: Time for yet another unhinged XL Wednesday CeleryDump for the good & bad people of Imgur
item.description:
greetings online friends
finally
if you know, you know
only choice
mmm hmm
off putting
I have questions
kinda cramped over there
understandable
worst, or best?
why are the trees so weird
everything?
we are about to find out...
used to.. sigh
clever
wait what
that dude needs to relax though
that's my set.. have a great night gang!!!
item.dc:creator: CeleryMan7

item.title: Life is odd sometimes
item.description: There's not much story to tell. Girl did it by accident in the kitchen. Tumor got already removed. I should be fine in a few weeks. Life is weird.

item.title: 1.5 years of HRT! Life is so full of color and joy now!
item.description: I hope the selfie will be excused this time! My "before" photo is 8 weeks into HRT and I was just beginning to experience how awesome life could be after decades of hating myself. My "after" photos are 1.5 years in, taken by my lovely girlfriend! Life is nothing short of incredible now!! I'm 68 days away from bottom surgery and SO excited!!! Cat tax paid on the third photo!
item.dc:creator: lurker2112

item.title: I'm proud of myself! Future finally looks brighter.
item.description: Posting for self reflection later on the road. I had been drinking for about 5 years, and used nicotine for 14 years. Now stopped both cold-turkey. Serious cravings for both. Stayed strong. It is starting to get a bit easier.
item.dc:creator: LeGilberto

item.title: The Return of the Meme: 1249 (overencucumbered)
item.description:
It's morning. Let's get this bread.
Pippin tax! Today's message from Pippin: "Good morning. Good boy Pippin."
item.dc:creator: CodyBurkett

item.title: Weekend gazebo build for special customer.
item.description: So this customers husband had talked for years about building a gazebo, sadly he passed shortly before. The customer had called me and asked if id be willing to build this. The last picture is an update picture sent from the customer. Honored to be a part of this one. Sorry theres no early photos, i was really focused on getting this done for her.
item.dc:creator: ZeroHourCreations

item.title: Swords!
item.dc:creator: swordscomic

item.title: USC Article VI, Clause III: No religious test shall ever be required as a qualification to any office or public trust under the United States.
item.description: Believing with you that religion is a matter which lies solely between Man & his God, that he owes account to none other for his faith or his worship, that the legitimate powers of government reach actions only, & not opinions, I contemplate with sovereign reverence that act of the whole American people which declared that their legislature should "make no law respecting an establishment of religion, or prohibiting the free exercise thereof," thus building a wall of separation between Church & State. Adhering to this expression of the supreme will of the nation in behalf of the rights of conscience, I shall see with sincere satisfaction the progress of those sentiments which tend to restore to man all his natural rights, convinced he has no natural right in opposition to his social duties.
item.dc:creator: HeShallKnowYourWaysAsIfBornToThem

item.title: You don't need to have a high IQ to be smart
item.description:
I'm nervous about this post. It's different from my other posts with both the topic and the actual content. I'm hoping that I've made this interesting enough. If not, I'll try again! . Most of my posts marvel at the beauty of outer space, and the vast, incomprehensible universe surrounding our tiny blue dot of a planet. However, there’s another field of study that I love just a tiny pinch more: neuroscience. Once upon a time, I was studying to be a research neuropsychologist. I wanted to study cluster b personality disorders (antisocial, narcissistic, histrionic, borderline) specifically. While I did learn many things while studying the brain and cluster b personality disorders, I want to focus on something else: IQ and our perception of intelligence.
*Truth is that “neuro” actually means “nervous system”, but neuropsychology is more focused on the brain part of the nervous system, so I simplified it to make more sense.
There are 100 BILLION neurons in your brain, rapidly firing both chemical and electrical pulses in order to perform every function in your body. Your heart beat? Yes, your heart is the organ pumping your blood, but the actual rhythm is controlled by your brain stem. Breathing? Thank a section of your brain in the very back of your skull called “pons.” . Your brain is the reason you are able to read this, while blinking, and rolling your thumb across your phone screen so you can scroll up and down. EVERYTHING is controlled by this big, squishy organ safely encased in your skull. I post a lot about outer space, because it's more popular. It’s easy to make the universe sound poetic and fantastic, because we are naturally attracted to the sparkling wonder of the stars. But the reason we can see ANY celestial body in our sky, recognize them as stars, and feel a sense of fascination and awe, is because those 100 billion neurons in your brain are firing back and forth. It blows my mind. There is something so beautiful and incredible about the fact that we experience love, an almost spiritual sensation, due to our brain creating and processing a couple of chemicals (serotonin, dopamine, oxytocin). Everything we are is thanks to our brain, and the billions of cells and the different types of tissue (white and grey matter) it's composed of. Microscopic structures paint the bigger picture that is us, and our place in the world. Okay, ramble over. Thanks for indulging me for a second!
I think before we can get into why IQ is mostly irrelevant to true intelligence, we should first establish what IQ actually is. As a beginning note: my version is simplified. IQ measures A LOT of things, but the overview is logic and reason. There's actually a specific section of psychology dedicated to IQ and IQ testing, and it can get a little complicated. However, the overall information is correct! Provided my professors at Texas State University and online classes have taught me properly lol.
I failed to mention this: IQ = Intelligence Quotia It measures our brain's ability to be a computer. There is a certain amount of cognitive functioning that a person needs in order to properly function in the world. Cognitive functioning refers tothe ability to process information and use it properly (logic). It involves critical thinking, mathematical ability, language comprehension, and the ability to make connections (ex: the ability to look at a fish and a bowl of water, and identify that the fish lives in the bowl). IQ is measured through standardized testing, and some cognitive assessment. . There's a certain range of IQ scores. 160 and beyond: genius/savant 150 - 160: EXTREMELY highly intelligent 130 - 150: very highly intelligent 120 - 129: highly intelligent 110 - 120: above average intelligence 90 - 110: average intelligence 80 - 89: below average intelligence 70 - 79: borderline intellectually disabled 69 and below: severely intellectually disabled . IQ has its uses. It's not totally irrelevent. It's most useful when determining intellectual disability. Someone with an IQ of 70 or lower is going to need assistance, especially in school. Some IQs are so low that the person can't read or speak/comprehend full sentences. That's where the TBI comes into play as well. A traumatic brain injury can drastically lower your cognitive abilities. In this case, an IQ test would measure just how bad the injury damaged your cognitive functioning. It's also helpful for children with a higher IQ that might need more challenging schoolwork in order to be properly MENTALLY stimulated at school (I emphasize "mental stimulation" because that sentence would be horrifyingly creepy otherwise). All of these boil down to: when our cognitive abilities require extra care in one way or another. . Outside of those circumstances, that number is useless. Thnat score doesn't mean that you're incapable of learning and doing great things.
The reason that IQ is highly emphasized in our perception of intelligence is because we think that IQ affects our ability to learn. There is definitely truth in that, as mentioned previously - but we've hyperfocused on it. "I don't know any of that! I only have an IQ of 100!" It creates this notion that this number determines the amount of knowledge that we have. We think that an average IQ means you can't learn the things that a genius knows. Spoiler Alert: That's a myth. An IQ does not sentence you to [x] amount of knowledge. Let's say you're taking a physics course with a child genius. The child might have an easier time with grasping the information, but you're both learning the same thing. More than that, our brains aren't pieces of hardware like a computer. It's adaptable. It can acquire knowledge and skills, which increases our ability to learn.
I'm going to let you in on a little secret: even savants can be idiots. I had a high school classmate. He was a clinically diagnosed savant and a total idiot because he was too busy being obsessed with his high IQ in order to do anything with it. Being able to learn super fast means nothing if you're not bothering to try to learn anything. . Even more important than that: you can teach your brain to be more intelligent. Your brain is a block of clay. It adjusts its structure to how you mold it. That's what I mean by "intelligence is malleable." I wouldn't say that it gets "bigger" - that would be bad, because it needs to stay in your skull. Instead, it becomes more refined and efficient.
What you learn and how you use that new knowledge is where intelligence lies. Not some number on a standardized scale. That's all talk, though. I am going to use myself and my accomplishments as an example. It’s not to brag. It’s because I know my IQ score, which is relevant in this example. If I could use someone else as an example, I would've preferred to do that instead. I try to avoid being narcissistic as much as possible, because I know the damage that narcissism can couse. Please forgive me on this one!
I’ve been told that I’m EXTREMELY intelligent. I’ve made languages, help stop bad guys, restructured a form of cognitive behavioral therapy, composed my own choir pieces, had poetry published in books, and more. My IQ is average. It's not even 100! The high intelligence was simply learning new things and making use of that knowledge. Anyone can be highly intelligent, accomplished, and doing great things - even with an average IQ score. . Side Note: The thing with the foot support was really just a filler because the photo looked awkward otherwise. My physical therapist said that figuring out a way to make a shoe insert from clay was a genius idea, but she migh've also been saying that to make me feel better lol.
You don't need a high IQ to be smart. IQ is only truly relevant when it's impacting cognitive functioning. And honestly? Emotional IQ is the important one - the ability to recognize our emotions and handle them in a healthy and efficient manner. Yes, humans are defined by our higher cognitive function than other creatures in the animal kingdom. But we are also defined by our ability to feel and use empathy. Our best intelligence is our empathetic intelligence, which allows us to be the best version of ourselves.
I learned almost all of this in both online college courses, in person lessons at Texas State University, and intensive Dialectical Behavioral Therapy classes. However, I ALWAYS cite my sources, so I felt like I should include this image anyway. Instead, I'm going to compile some really interesting sources for anyone who wants to learn more about the brain! . This is my favorite 3D brain model. It shows the different structures of the brain, what they do, and interesting case studies: http://www.g2conline.org/3dbrain/ This is where I got the picture of the neuron from: https://www.researchgate.net/figure/Image-showing-real-neurons-from-the-human-brain-They-have-been-filled-with-a-fluorescent_fig1_271556079 This is a YouTube playlist that acts like a mini-college course on its own. I will warn you that it has dense language, because it's a series of real college seminars: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nlSL7Qg7-Po&list=PLeRCSVJpV37Iqv6w0p8hiuCYTBLutCqId&pp=iAQB Here's an interesting overview of IQ, but the rest of it has a paywall: https://study.com/academy/lesson/methods-of-measuring-intelligence-interpreting-an-iq-scores-score-range.html#:~:text=Lesson%20Summary,-In%20summary%2C%20IQ&text=The%20concept%20of%20IQ%20was,of%20scores%20will%20be%2015. A little bit about emotional intelligence: https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/emotional-intelligence#:~:text=Emotional%20intelligence%20(EI)%20refers%20to,found%20in%20important%20life%20outcomes. If any expert wants to add their own, or tweak this post, feel free! :)
item.dc:creator: OlivetheNerd
